segments.s437-g73: بگفتند is the past plural 'they said'. و links the clauses ('and'). هر دو برابر شدند literally 'each two became برابر' — here برابر is used in the sense 'matched / equal / facing one another', and شدند is the past plural 'they became/stood'. Word order is subject-verb (they said) then 'هر دو' (the two) + predicate (برابر شدند).
segments.s437-g74: به اندیشه is an adverbial phrase 'in/with respect to thought/wisdom' (به + noun). از marks the comparative 'than' (here introducing the standard of comparison). ماه means 'moon' and برتر is the comparative/adjective 'superior/better'. شدند is again past plural 'they became' — together: 'they became superior to the moon in thought'.
segments.s437-g75: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'see'. تا here introduces a purposive/indirect exhortation 'so that / to see' (common in older/persian poetic usage: 'look to see what...'). چه is the interrogative 'what'. گفته‌ست is the perfect/past form 'has said'. مرد خرد is a genitive phrase 'the man of wisdom' (mard + khord 'wisdom' used as epithet).
segments.s437-g76: که introduces the reported/pronounced statement ('that' / 'for'). هرکس که is the correlative 'whoever' (هرکس + relative که). بد کرد is simple past 'did bad/evil'. کیفر برد is an idiomatic clause 'took/received punishment' — برد here is 'receive/take' in context (not 'victory'); it behaves as the main verb in past tense.
segments.s437-g77: شبی is an adverbial 'one night' / 'by night'. تا here functions temporally 'when/until' (when the sun rose). برآمد is past 'rose/appeared'. ز + کوه is the poetic 'from the mountain'. آفتاب is 'the sun'. The clause gives temporal setting: 'one night, when the sun rose from the mountain'.
segments.s437-g78: دو تن را: دو تن 'the two persons' with را marking them as the definite/direct object/topic. سر اندر نیامد به خواب is an idiomatic negative: literally 'sleep did not come into (their) heads' meaning 'they could not sleep' — سر = 'head', اندر = 'into/within', نیامد = 'did not come', به خواب = 'into sleep'. The verb is in the past negative form (نیامد).
segments.s437-g79: که introduces the content/purpose ('that' / 'so that'). ما is the subject 'we'. نام او از جهان کم کنیم is a clause 'we make his name small from the world' — idiomatically 'we will diminish his reputation in the world'. کم کنیم is present-subjunctive/optative 'we may lessen / let us lessen' (کنیم used as hortative/intentional).
segments.s437-g80: دل و دیده زال is a possessive grouping 'the heart and the eye(s) of Zāl' (noun1 و noun2 + owner). پر نم کنیم: پر is 'full', نم is 'dew/tears' and کنیم is again the subjunctive/hortative 'we make/fill' — together 'we will fill Zāl's heart and eyes full of tears'. Word order places the possessed body-parts before the verb phrase.
segments.s437-g137: ز here is the archaic/poetic preposition meaning 'because of / at (the sight of)'. دیدار او is a genitive phrase 'the seeing/meeting of him'. شاد شد is 'became happy' (شاد = predicate adjective; شد = the verb 'became') and پهلوان is the subject placed after the predicate and verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s437-g138: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دیدش is دید + object suffix -ش 'he saw him'. The adjectives خردمند و روشن‌روان are predicative descriptions of the person seen (elliptical: 'when he saw him (that he was) wise and bright‑souled'). The clause is subordinate and describes the moment/reason of the previous rejoicing.
segments.s437-g139: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین introducing reported speech). کز is a contracted classical form (که + از) meaning 'from/of the stock of'. تخمه سام شیر is a genitive chain 'a lion of/from the seed (line) of Sam' with تخمه (offspring) governing the name and شیر.
segments.s437-g140: نزاید is the (classical/poetic) verb 'be born / is produced' (here with negative sense 'is not born' when read with the preceding clause). مگر introduces an exception ('except / unless'). زورمند و دلیر are the qualities that the exception allows ('mighty and brave'). The clause means '...is not born except (one) mighty and brave.'
segments.s437-g141: چگونه است is an interrogative 'how is'. کار تو 'your affair / business' is the subject/phrase being asked about. با کابلی is a prepositional phrase 'with a Kabuli/Kabulite' specifying the other party. Word order is neutral/question: question word + copula + nominal phrase + prepositional complement.
segments.s437-g142: چه is the interrogative 'what'. گویند is the archaic/poetic third‑person plural 'they say' (poetic present). از رستم زابلی is the prepositional phrase 'about/of Rostam the Zabli' (زابلī = 'of Zabul'), so the whole clause asks 'what do people say about Rostam of Zabul?'.
segments.s437-g143: چنین داد پاسخ = 'thus (he) gave (an) answer'. داد is the verb 'gave' and پاسخ is the direct object 'answer'. به رستم marks the indirect object 'to Rostam'. شغاد at the end is the subject (name) placed after the clause — a common poetic inversion: 'Shaghād gave such an answer to Rostam.'
segments.s437-g144: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'saying that'). از شاه کابل = 'of/about the king of Kabul'. مکن is the negative imperative/jussive 'do not (do)'. نیز یاد literally 'also/indeed mention' — together مکن یاد is an idiomatic prohibition 'do not mention' (here 'do not even mention the king of Kabul').
segments.s437-g81: چنین (thus) is an adverb placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the simple past verb 'said' with the subject following it in the classical Persian order; با + شاه کابل functions as the indirect object/recipient (here 'to the king of Kabul'), and شغاد is the speaker's name placed after the predicate.
segments.s437-g82: که‌گر is the archaic fused conditional 'که گر' meaning 'if'. زین is a poetic/archaic form ('this' or 'from this') linking to سخن 'word/matter'. The phrase داد خواهیم داد repeats داد: the first داد functions as the nominal idea 'justice' or 'recompense' and خواهیم داد is the periphrastic future 'we will give' (خواهیم + verb), so the clause means 'if by this speech we must give justice, we will give it.'
segments.s437-g83: یکی is an indefinite/numeral ('one' / 'a certain') used here to introduce an action; سور is 'feast/banquet' and کن is the imperative 'do/make' (سور کن = 'make a feast'). مهتران را is the definite plural object ('the elders/chiefs'), with را marking the object, and به خوان 'to the table/banquet' gives the destination or occasion.
segments.s437-g84: می‌و here is an archaic/poetic cluster related to 'mi' (wine) plus connective; رود is the musical/reed instrument (or a poetic name for a string/woodwind instrument) and stands with می‌و as things to be brought/played. و is coordinating 'and'; رامشگران را is the definite object 'the entertainers/peacemakers' (را marks them) and بخوان is the imperative of خواندن here meaning 'call/summon' or 'make them sing/perform.'
segments.s437-g85: به introduces the circumstance 'in/while' (here 'in drinking'); می‌خوردن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'drinking' (classical mi‑ + خوردن), اندر مرا gives the direction/target 'toward me' (اندر = 'at/in/among', مرا = 'me' with را split earlier), سرد is an adjective 'cold/harsh', and گوی is an imperative/command form 'say' or 'call' (classical use of گوی for 'say/declare'). The clause means 'while you drink, speak to me coldly / call me so.'
segments.s437-g86: میان is the preposition 'among' or 'in the midst of'; کیان is the proper collective 'the Kians' (the royal house/kings); ناجوانمرد is an adjective 'dishonourable / unmanly'; and گوی again commands 'say/call' — together: 'call me dishonourable among the Kians.'
segments.s437-g87: ز is the poetic/genitive preposition 'from/because of'; خواری is the noun 'humiliation/shame'; شوم is the first‑person present/future of شدن used here 'I will be / I shall become / I will go (in consequence)' — in context it carries the sense 'I will (leave)'; سوی introduces the direction 'toward', and زابلستان is the place name 'Zabulistan.' The line means 'out of shame I will go to Zabulistan.'
segments.s437-g88: بنالم is the first‑person present/future of نالیدن 'to lament/complain' (I will lament / I complain); ز is again 'because of'; سالار is 'commander/lord/chief'; کابلستان is the territory 'Kabulistan.' The clause reads 'I will lament because of the lord (the chief) of Kabulistan.'
segments.s437-g185: چنین (an adverb) + گفت (past of گفتن) + رستم is an archaic/poetic V–S order: 'thus said Rostam.' The particle که introduces reported speech or a clause that follows. اینست is the fused poetic form of این است ('this is'); راه is the predicate noun following the copula (modern order would be این راه است). Altogether: 'thus Rostam said that this is the way.'
segments.s437-g186: The phrase uses an older word order and some fused elements: مرا خود = 'for me, myself' (مرا is the object/emphatic form, with خود emphasizing 'myself'); به کابل = 'to Kabul' (به + place); نباید is an impersonal/modal verb meaning 'ought not / is not necessary'; سپاه = 'army' (noun). The sense is: 'As for me personally, an army is not necessary to go to Kabul.'
segments.s437-g187: زواره (a place-name) + بس (postpositive 'enough' / 'sufficient') gives 'Zowāreh is enough' or 'from Zowāreh that will do.' و links items. نامور precedes the numeral phrase here as a descriptive adjective ('renowned'); صد سوار = 'a hundred horsemen.' Poetic word order places the adjective before the numeral and noun.
segments.s437-g188: پیاده here is a noun meaning 'infantry/foot-soldiers' rather than the adverb 'on foot.' همان نیز = 'the same also' (همان picks up the same quantity/quality just stated). صد نامدار = 'a hundred renowned ones.' The segment repeats the previous quota for foot soldiers: 'and likewise a hundred renowned foot soldiers.'
segments.s437-g129: همی رفت is a poetic past-progressive: همی + past-verb indicates he 'was going' (a continuing past action). با introduces an accompaniment; کابلی چند مرد literally 'Kabulī several men' — in poetry the quantifier چند follows the noun, so this phrase means 'a few men from Kabul' (مرد = 'men'). Word order is inverted for metrical/poetic effect but reads as 'he went with several Kabulites.'
segments.s437-g130: This is a pair of descriptive noun-phrases glued together: دلی پر ز کین = 'a heart full of hatred' (پر ز + noun = 'full of ...'), and لب پر از باد سرد = 'lips full of cold wind'. Persian poetic style often omits a copula; these phrases qualify the (foregoing) man. Note the two parallel uses پر ز / پر از ('full of') and the concrete image باد سرد = 'cold wind.'
segments.s437-g131: بیامد is the simple past 'he came.' به درگاه = 'to the court/presence of'; فرخ پدر is a poetic adjective + noun ('fortunate father'), where the epithet فرخ precedes or accompanies پدر to describe whose court it is. The whole phrase is a prepositional complement of بیامد: 'he came to the court of the fortunate father.'
segments.s437-g132: Again two parallel 'full-of' phrases: دلی پر ز چاره = 'a heart full of chāreh/expedients' (i.e. full of schemes or plans), and سر پر از کینه = 'a head full of grudge/hatred.' The order places دلی... first then سر...; Persian here omits a linking verb and stacks the adjectival phrases to describe his inner state on arrival.
segments.s437-g133: هم‌انگه 'at that moment' + چو used temporally as 'when.' The clause چو روی پسر دید زال literally places the verb دید before the subject زال (a common poetic inversion): 'when Zal saw the son's face.' The sequence is temporal marker → subordinate 'when' clause → verb → subject for metrical effect.
segments.s437-g134: چنان 'so/such' introduces a list of qualities: برز و بالا = 'lofty/high' (two synonymous epithets), and آن فر و یال = 'that glory and mane' (آن pointing to the remarkable splendour). The adjectives and nouns are coordinated with و; this is a poetic accumulation of attributes describing the son Zal saw.
segments.s437-g135: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (polished literary past). بسیار here modifies پرسیدن to mean 'asked much' or 'asked many questions.' The conjunction و links the next verb; بنواختش is نواخت with the prefix بـ and the object clitic -ش, 'struck him' (in context physical striking, not musical), again in past tense.
segments.s437-g136: هم‌انگه 'at that instant' resumes the narrative; بر + پیلتن marks the target ('against Piltan') and تاختش is past of تاختن with the object clitic -ش, here 'attacked/charged at him' (poetic past). The preposition بر means 'against' in the sense of 'rushed upon/attacked.'
segments.s437-g89: چه ... چه is a correlative pair meaning 'whether ... or' (an emphatic either/or). پیش here means 'in the presence of / before' (spatial/social: in front of someone). The two phrases 'پیش برادر' and 'پیش پدر' are parallel complements to the main clause that follows.
segments.s437-g90: ترا is the older two-part form of 'تو را' (object 'you'); را is the definite/object marker attached after the pronoun while the actual object is 'سر' in other lines but here marks 'you' as the object of خوانم. ناسزا خوانم is a light-verb construction: ناسزا (abuse/insult) + خوانم ('I call/I will call') = 'I will call (you) abusive names' (خوانم is 1st person present/jussive, here performative). The conjunction و links a second predicate 'بدگهر' (an insulting noun/adjective) that is understood as also being predicated of 'you' (i.e. 'and [call you] bad‑guy / vile').
segments.s437-g91: برآشوبد is a 3rd‑person jussive/optative meaning 'may he be disturbed / may it be upset.' The sequence 'او را سر' uses او + را where را marks the following noun 'سر' as the definite object ('his head'). 'از بهر من' is an adverbial phrase 'on account of me / for my sake' (از بهر = because of/for the sake of; من = me). Together: 'May his head be disturbed for my sake.'
segments.s437-g92: بیاید is another jussive/optative 3rd‑person form 'may he come.' بدین here functions as 'to this' (classical compound of به + این = 'to/into this'); نامور شهر من = 'my renowned/famed city' with من marking possessive ('of mine'). The phrase as a whole continues the optative wish: 'may he come to this famous city of mine.'
segments.s437-g93: برآید is a 3rd‑person present/jussive used conditionally here ('should it come about / if it happens'). چنین کار = 'such a deed / such a thing.' بر دست ما is an idiomatic locative 'into our hands / in our control' (بر + دست = 'upon the hand', i.e. under our control).
segments.s437-g94: به چرخ فلک‌بر بود شست ما is a compact word order: شستِ ما ('our thumb' — شست here = 'thumb') is the possessor phrase, and به چرخ فلک‌بر بود = 'was upon/over the wheel of fate/heaven.' چرخ فلک (wheel of the sky) is an idiom for fate; فلک‌بر qualifies it ('fate‑striking / of the heavens'). بود is past/copular 'was.' Overall: 'the wheel of heaven was under our thumb' (i.e. we controlled fate).
segments.s437-g95: تو is the vocative/subject 'you.' نخچیرگاهی is the locative 'hunting‑ground.' نگه کن is an imperative compound: نگه (watch/keeping) + کن (imperative 'do') = 'keep watch / stand guard.' به راه is a locative adjunct 'on the road / along the path' specifying where to watch.
segments.s437-g96: بکن is the imperative 'do / make / dig.' چاه here means 'pit / trap' (a dug hole for trapping). چندی is an adverb 'for a while / some time.' به نخچیرگاه marks the goal/location 'at/to the hunting‑ground.' Together: 'Dig a pit (trap) for a while at the hunting‑ground.'
segments.s437-g113: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. The clause follows the common Persian order Subject (سر) + predicate (پر شد) + adjunct (از باده خسروی): سر پر شد is a perfective/stative 'became/full'; از + noun expresses the thing causing the fullness ('filled with wine'); خسروی is an adjectival postmodifier 'kingly/royal' modifying باده.
segments.s437-g114: This is a main clause naming the agent (شغاد) followed by the locative/inner particle اندر and the verb/adjective آشفت 'became upset, was disturbed'. از introduces the cause ('because of') and is followed by the compound noun بدخوی 'ill-tempered'. Note the slightly archaic inward locative اندر instead of modern در/درون.
segments.s437-g115: چنین 'thus' opens the speech; گفت here functions as a speech verb but the provided lexical senses list is for the noun—its role is verbal 'he said'. با شاه کابل is the prepositional phrase 'to/with the king of Kabul' (classical با can mark the addressee). که introduces the reported content (direct speech) and is followed by the first-person pronoun من.
segments.s437-g116: همی is an older/prosodic form marking continuous or habitual aspect 'always'. سرفرازم is predicative (I am proud/victorious); Persian places the predicative adjective after the subject and often before circumstantial phrases. به هر انجمن is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/at every assembly' (به + noun), with هر 'every' modifying انجمن.
segments.s437-g117: برادر is a vocative or nominative noun ('brother') used as the head of the phrase. Two consecutive چو clauses (چو رستم چو دستان پدر) supply similes: چو = 'like/as', so the structure reads 'a brother like Rostam, like Dastan's father' (both comparisons modify برادر). Word order is noun + comparative similes, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s437-g118: ازین ... تر is the comparative construction 'more ... than this/than him'; از (from/than) + این (this) + تر (comparative ending implied on the adjective) yields 'more ... than this'. نامورتر is the comparative 'more renowned'. که introduces a relative clause ('who/which') modifying the implied standard, and دارد گهر is present-tense possession 'has/possesses a jewel'.
segments.s437-g119: ازو (= از او / از این) functions adverbially as 'thereupon'/'on that account'. شاه کابل is the title + place-name (king of Kabul) as subject. برآشفت is a past-tense/preterite verb meaning 'became angry' and و گفت links to the following quoted or reported clause ('and said').
segments.s437-g120: که introduces the content of what was said (a reported/interrogative clause). چندین is an indefinite/adverbial 'many' or 'many a', چه is the interrogative 'what' (here working together as 'how many things' or 'what many things'), داری is present 'you have/you hold', سخن is the object 'words/speech', and در نهفت is a compound predicate meaning 'hidden in' (در + نهفت 'hidden'), so the clause asks roughly 'what many things do you have hidden in your speech?'.
segments.s437-g17: بگفتند is a past, third‑person plural reporting verb ('they said') placed at the clause start to introduce reported speech. با here marks the addressee (classical usage akin to 'to/with'); 'زال سام سوار' is the addressee in apposition (Zāl, called 'Sam the rider'), where سام is a proper name and سوار is a descriptive apposition 'rider/horseman'.
segments.s437-g18: که‌ای combines the relative/particle که with the vocative ای (classical/poetic) and functions as 'O you who...' or 'who is...'; از introduces the genitive 'of/from', بلند is an adjective 'lofty/high' modifying اختران ('stars'); یادگار is a noun 'memento/memorial' used here as 'remnant' or 'memorial' of the stars. Note: اختران is the plural of اختر 'star' (the provided sense list did not include 'star').
segments.s437-g19: گرفتیم is first‑person plural past 'we took/seized/undertook'; و links the two verbs; جستیم here is used in the sense 'we searched/sought (out)' rather than 'leapt'; راز سپهر is a genitive phrase 'the secret/mystery of the sky/heavens' with سپهر meaning 'heavens/sky'.
segments.s437-g20: ندارد is third‑person singular present 'has not' or 'there is not'; بدین is the adverbial/locative 'بدین' = 'for/by/with this' (here split in the word list as ب + دین), introducing the phrase that follows; کودک 'child' and خرد 'wisdom/intelligence' form the noun phrase 'this child's wisdom'; مهر is 'affection/love' meaning 'there is no affection/mercy for this child's wisdom' (i.e. the heavens show no favour).
segments.s437-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; این خوب‌چهره is the demonstrative plus epithet 'this fair‑faced (one)'; به marks goal/attainment 'to/into', and مردی here means 'manhood/manliness' (i.e. coming of age); رسد is present/subjunctive/epic present 'reaches/attains' (used as a general statement about the future event).
segments.s437-g22: به گاه is an adverbial expression 'at the time/when'; دلیری 'bravery/valor' and گردی literally 'dust' here denote 'tumult/commotion' (a poetic image for battle/uproar); و links the two nouns; رسد repeats the verb 'arrives/comes' — the clause says the time of valor and tumult will come when he attains manhood.
segments.s437-g23: کند is the present/future‑oriented poetic verb 'makes/does' (here causative: 'will make'); تخمه سام is 'Sam's seed/offspring' (تخمه used as 'offspring'); نیرم is an adjective 'weak/feeble' and تباه 'ruined/destroyed' — both postpositive adjectives describe the fate inflicted on Sam's progeny.
segments.s437-g24: شکست is the noun 'defeat/failure'; اندرآرد is a causative verb 'brings in/causes' (here 'causes defeat'); بدین (split as ب + دین in the word list) is an instrumental/adverbial 'by/with this' referring to the scheme just mentioned; دستگاه is 'system/scheme/arrangement' (here 'this scheme/plan').
segments.s437-g41: بران سال is a poetic temporal phrase meaning 'in/that year' (بران = بر + آن). کودک is the subject 'the child' and برافراخت is a past tense verb 'raised/erected'; یال is the direct object 'mane' (here a metaphor for reaching youth or growing hair). Note the slightly marked word order: subject (کودک) + verb (برافراخت) + object (یال) rather than strict SOV.
segments.s437-g42: بر شاه کابل is a dative/goal phrase 'to the king of Kabul' (بر = 'to/toward' here) placed before the verb. فرستاد is the simple past 'sent/dispatch(ed)' and زال is the agent, placed after the verb by poetic inversion (verb–subject order). The implicit object ('him') refers back to the child.
segments.s437-g43: جوان شد is 'he became a youth/young man' (شد = became). به بالای سرو بلند is a locative/measure phrase 'to the top/height of a tall cypress' describing his growth or stature; به + بالای gives the sense 'up to/onto the top of'. Word order places the predicate before the measure phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s437-g44: سواری دلاور is a noun phrase 'a brave horseman' (noun + epithet). به گرز و کمند is an instrumental phrase 'with (or armed with) mace and lasso' where به functions adverbially/instrumentally ('by/with'). The verbless juxtaposition is typical of epic lines: two nominal phrases linked to describe the young man.
segments.s437-g45: سپهدار کابل is the subject 'the commander of Kabul'. بدو is the clitic/prepositional 'to him' and بنگرید is a narrative past/presentive form meaning 'looked upon' or 'regarded'. The verb precedes no expressed subject here (subject appears earlier), which is normal in poetic narration.
segments.s437-g46: همی is an emphatic adverb ('indeed' / 'surely') and تاج و تخت کیان را is the definite object 'the crown and throne of the Kayanids' (را marks the object). سزید is a past-tense impersonal/active 'befitted / he deserved' — together meaning that those honors were fitting for him. The verb-final idea is rendered compactly and poetically.
segments.s437-g47: به گیتی is a locative 'in the world' (به + گیتی). به دیدار او is a prepositional phrase 'at his sight / on seeing him' (به = 'towards/at' + دیدار = 'seeing/appearance'). بود شاد is 'was happy/joyful' (بود = was, شاد = happy). Together: 'The world was glad at seeing him.'
segments.s437-g48: بدو داد is 'gave to him' (بدو = to him, داد = gave), with the giver understood from the previous line (the commander). دختر is the object 'a daughter'. ز بهر نژاد is a purpose/reason phrase 'for the sake of lineage' (ز = 'because of/for', بهر = 'for/sake', نژاد = 'lineage'). The clause states that he bestowed his daughter on him because of his noble line.
segments.s437-g169: This phrase is a compressed, poetic order for ‘he chose a worthy man from the army.’ ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from' marking source; لشگر (army) is the source noun. گزین کرد is a compound past (گزین + کرد) meaning 'he chose' (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made) and شایسته modifies مرد ('a worthy/fit man'). The object marker را is omitted here because of the terse poetic word order.
segments.s437-g170: کسی را is the noun phrase 'someone' with را as the definite/direct-object/contrast marker; که introduces a relative clause modifying کسی. نامی بود is a past-tense copular clause 'was famed/notable' and در نبرد is a locative phrase 'in battle' specifying the field in which he was known.
segments.s437-g171: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past verb 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause 'so that' or 'in order that.' ساز رفتن کردن is a literary idiom: ساز here means 'preparation, means' (not a musical instrument) and رفتن کنند uses the present subjunctive کنند after تا to express what they should do ('make ready to go').
segments.s437-g172: This gives source and destination with the poetic preposition ز 'from' and به 'to.' ز زابل به کابل indicates movement 'from Zabul to Kabul,' and نشیمن کنند is a subjunctive verb phrase ('they should take up a seat/dwelling') with کنند matching the subordinate clause introduced by تا.
segments.s437-g173: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. شد is the past 'became/was' and here works with ساخته (past participle) to mean 'was made/was completed.' کار لشکر همه ساخته therefore means 'the army's work/all the army's affairs having been made/done' (i.e., when the army's business was finished).
segments.s437-g174: دل پهلوان is a possessive noun phrase 'the hero's/champion's heart.' گشت is a past intransitive 'became/turned,' and پرداخته is a past participle used adjectivally/poetically meaning 'stirred/affected' (here: emotionally moved or agitated). The whole line says the champion's heart became moved.
segments.s437-g175: بیامد is the simple past 'came.' بر here functions as a directional/prepositional particle meaning 'toward/to' and introduces the noun phrase مرد جنگی شغاد, where مرد جنگی is 'a warlike/man of war' and شغاد is a proper name in apposition. In classical Persian names often follow the descriptive noun.
segments.s437-g176: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding man. با شاه کابل 'with the king of Kabul' gives the partner/agent. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' and رزم یاد is an idiomatic literary object where رزم (battle) + یاد (remembrance/recall) together mean 'take up arms / make war'; here the clause forbids waging war on the king of Kabul. This is an example of an archaic idiom (object before the verb) common in epic diction.
segments.s437-g57: 'در اندیشه X' is a locative/possessive phrase meaning 'in the thought/mind of X'. Word order is noun 'اندیشه' (thought) followed by the possessor 'مهتر کابلی' (the Kabul chief). 'در' is the locative preposition 'in'.
segments.s437-g58: چنان بد = 'so/such bad' (here 'bad' = hostile/ill‑disposed). کزو is an older contracted particle (که + ز + او or 'از که/که ز') functioning as 'toward/from whom' linking the preceding clause to the person named; the proper name 'رستم زابلی' follows as its referent (Rostam of Zabul).
segments.s437-g59: نگیرد is negative present/habitual ('he does not/would not take' or 'he would not accept'); here it means 'refused to take/accept'. ز is the poetic 'from'. کار درم = 'a dirham from the work' (درم = dirham, i.e. a small sum). نیز here has an emphatic 'even/anymore' sense and یـاد (literally 'memory/trace') is tied to the idea of keepsake/remembrance of payment — 'not even a dirham's trace/claim'.
segments.s437-g60: ازان پس = 'after that / from that time on'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when'. داماد او شد شغاد is a standard verb clause 'Shaghad became his son‑in‑law' (داماد = son‑in‑law; او = 'his'; شد = 'became'; شغاد is the name/appositive). Word order places the result verb after the subject phrase, as is normal.
segments.s437-g61: چو = 'when'. هنگام باژ آمد = 'the time/moment of the cry/alarm came' (هنگام = at the time; باژ = alarm/cry; آمد = came). آن بستدند: آن = 'that/then' and بستدند is an archaic past plural meaning 'they tied/closed/ceased' — here 'they stopped/put an end to it' is the best reading.
segments.s437-g62: همه کابلستان = 'all Kabulistan' (subject). بهم بر زدند = 'they struck/attacked together' — بهم = 'together', بر + زدند = verb 'to strike/attack upon' (prefix بر gives the sense 'upon/against'), so the phrase means 'rose/attacked as one'.
segments.s437-g63: دژم شد = 'became sullen/grim' (دژم = sullen; شد = became). ز کار = 'because of the deed/act' (ز = because of/from; کار = deed). برادر شغاد can be read as 'Shaghad's brother' or 'the brother Shaghad' used appositively; syntactically it links the deed to that person (here read as 'the deed of Shaghad's brother').
segments.s437-g64: نکرد = 'did not do' (here 'did not say/utter'). آن سخن = 'that speech/word'. پیش کس = 'before anyone' or 'in the presence of anyone' (prepositional phrase). نیز یاد at the end expresses 'nor was it remembered / nor did anyone remember it' — یاد = 'memory/remembrance'. The clause thus states that he did not speak that word before anyone, and it was not remembered.
segments.s437-g105: سر چاه را سخت کن زان سپس is an imperative clause. سر چاه (lit. “head/top of the well”) is the direct object marked by را immediately after the noun (noun + را = definite object). سخت کن is an imperative: سخت (adjective/adverb ‘firm/hard’) + کن (imperative ‘make/do’). زان سپس is a temporal connector ‘after that / thereupon’ placed after the command to show sequence.
segments.s437-g106: مگوی این سخن نیز با هیچ‌کس is a negative imperative: مگوی = م‑ (negation) + گو (tell/say) in the negative imperative ‘do not tell/say’. این سخن ‘these words/this speech’ is the object; نیز here functions as an adverbial ‘also/too’ (emphatic: don’t tell it to anyone at all). با هیچ‌کس = ‘with no one’ (i.e. to no one), where با is the preposition and هیچ‌کس is the negated person.
segments.s437-g107: بشد شاه و رای از منش دور کرد begins with بشد, an older/poetic past or resultive verb (بَشد = ‘it happened / he became / rose’). شاه is the logical subject following this verbal form. و links the clauses (‘and/then’). رای از منش دور کرد is a causative/result clause: رای = ‘counsel/plan’, از منش = ‘from (his) nature/character’, دور کردن = ‘to put/remove (far)’, and کرد is the past auxiliary/causative ‘did/caused’ (دور کرد = ‘removed’).
segments.s437-g108: به گفتار آن بی‌خرد سور کرد: به introduces the domain/reason here (’by/at/for the speech’), گفتار = ‘speech/words’, آن بی‌خرد = ‘that fool’ (آن is the demonstrative), and سور کرد = ‘held/caused a feast’ (سور = feast, کرد = made/held). Grammatically this clause gives the motive/result tied to the preceding action.
segments.s437-g109: مهان را سراسر ز کابل بخواند: مهان (‘guests’) is marked as definite object by را. سراسر ز کابل = ‘throughout/from all parts of Kabul’ (ز is the poetic form of از = ‘from’). بخواند is a past narrative verb (3rd person), here meaning ‘summoned/called’ (in this context).
segments.s437-g110: بخوان پسندیده‌شان برنشاند links two coordinated actions. بَخوان here functions as a narrative verb ‘he sang/recited’ (poetic past) and is coordinated with برنشاند (‘he seated/placed’). پسندیده‌شان contains the possessive clitic ‑شان (‘their/his’) attached to پسندیده (‘chosen/approved’). برنشاند is a past verb meaning ‘set/placed/ seated’. The sequence describes performance followed by seating of the favored guests.
segments.s437-g111: چو نان خورده شد مجلس آراستند: چو = ‘when’; نان خورده شد is a circumstantial clause (‘bread having been eaten’), where خورده (past participle ‘eaten’) + شد functions as periphrastic past/result. مجلس (the gathering/assembly) is the subject of آراستند (past plural ‘they arranged/adorned’). The clause gives the time and the ensuing action.
segments.s437-g112: می‌و رود و رامشگران خواستند: this lists what followed: می‌و رود is a paired noun phrase (می/ wine or fruit and رود/ a stringed instrument in this poetic idiom), joined by و ‘and’. رامشگران (entertainers/comforters) is the object of خواستند (past plural ‘they desired/they called/they summoned’). Grammatically, action verbs are in past plural to match the narrative subject.
segments.s437-g161: ازو نیز مندیش is an instruction to take (or think) 'also' regarding someone: ازو (about/of him) + نیز (also). مندیش is a poetic/archaic verb form (here treated as an imperative or jussive meaning 'consider/think of'); it links as the main directive governing the following phrase وز لشکرش. Word order is topic (about him) + adverb + verb.
segments.s437-g162: که introduces the content clause that follows ('that' / 'namely'). مه is an archaic/intensifying adjective here meaning 'great' (not the celestial 'moon'). باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (wishing); و simply links the two wishes. افسرش is the possessed noun 'his commander/officer' (or helmet in some contexts), parallel to 'his army'. The clause thus expresses two parallel wishes: 'may his army be great and may his officer be great.'
segments.s437-g163: من ... کنم is a first‑person present/future construction ('I will ...' or 'I will make'). او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks the direct object). بدین گفته is an instrumental/circumstantial phrase 'by/with this saying' (ب = prepositional prefix 'by/with/to', دین+گفته together mean 'this statement/these words'); بیجان کنم is the verb phrase 'I will render lifeless' (بیجان = lifeless, کنم = 'I cause/make').
segments.s437-g164: برو here functions as 'I will go' / 'I go' in sequence with the following action (in epic Persian simple verb forms often narrate immediate intent). بر دل is a locative 'upon/to the heart', دوده پیچان is a compound participial/adjectival phrase 'wrapping/enshrouding with soot/smoke' (دوده = soot/smoke, پیچان = wrapping/twining). کنم repeats the first‑person action 'I will do/make', so the whole clause reads 'I will go and wrap soot around his heart.'
segments.s437-g165: ترا is the object 'you' (here shown as the enclitic ت + object marker را); برنشانم is a compound verb 'I will seat (someone) upon' (بر + نشانم), so the clause literally 'I will seat you upon his throne.' اوی after تخت is the possessive 'his.' Note the archaic attachment of the 2nd‑person suffix to را (ت + را = ترا).
segments.s437-g166: به خاک اندر آرم is a verbal phrase meaning 'I will put/bring into the dust' (به ... اندر 'into/onto the earth' + آرم = I place / I let rest, an older 1st‑person form). سر بخت اوی is a genitive phrase 'the head of his fortune' (سر = head, بخت = fortune/fate, اوی = his). The two parts form one continuous promise: 'and I will lay the head of his fate in the dust.'
segments.s437-g167: همی داشتش is an older continuous/past narrative form meaning 'he held/kept him' (همی marks continuous or habitual aspect). روز چند is a time phrase 'for some days/several days.' ارجمند is a predicate adjective 'esteemed' or 'honored' describing the way he was held. The comma signals that 'esteemed' qualifies his treatment during that time.
segments.s437-g168: سپرده here is a past participial/past sense 'entrusted' (i.e., 'he entrusted'), بدو means 'to him' (directional dative), and جایگاه بلند is the object 'a high/lofty place/position.' Together: 'he entrusted to him a high place/position.' Word order is past verb (or participle) + indirect object + direct object.
segments.s437-g49: ز گنج بزرگ آنچ بد در خورش is a prepositional phrase starting with the poetic preposition ز (from). گنج بزرگ is a noun phrase 'great treasure/treasury'. آنچ (آن‌چ = آنچه) is a relative/indefinite pronoun 'that which/whatever'. بد here is an archaic compressed form (contextually acting like بود ‘was’), introducing what he possessed; در + خورش is a locative 'in/within ...' that links to آنچ to mean 'that which was in (his) stores.'
segments.s437-g50: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent/dispatched' and is the main verb of the clause. با is the preposition 'with' linking the sent goods to the companion, and نامور دخترش is the noun phrase 'his famed/renowned daughter' with the pronominal suffix -ش on دختر marking 'his daughter.'
segments.s437-g51: همی داشتش is a continuous/iterative imperfective construction where همی gives an ongoing sense ('he used to / he continually') and داشت (past stem) takes the clitic -ش as its object: 'he held/cherished her.' چون یکی تازه سیب is a simile 'like a fresh apple' (چون = like; یکی = a; تازه = fresh; سیب = apple).
segments.s437-g52: کز = 'that/which' introduces a relative clause. اختر 'star' is the comparison domain. نبودی is an archaic past-form (بود + negative pattern here functioning as 'was not' or 'there was not') and بروبر literally means 'equal/face-to-face' (i.e. 'matched'). نهیب in this line is idiomatic/poetic and does not carry its ordinary senses straightforwardly; together the clause says there was no equal to her among the stars.
segments.s437-g53: بزرگان ایران و هندوستان is a coordination: بزرگان 'the great ones/elders' followed by the coordinated geographical nouns ایران and هندوستان (Iran and India/Hindustan). Word order is noun – place modifiers, joined by و 'and'.
segments.s437-g54: ز here is the poetic preposition 'about/of/from' introducing the topic 'Rostam' (ز رستم). زدندی is a past plural verb from زدن; in epic usage زدن + داستان often means 'to recite/tell' (literally 'they struck/told'), so 'they told/struck out (tales)'. همی is the iterative/continuous adverb (here stylistic), and داستان is the object 'story/tale/legend.' The clause therefore means 'they told tales of Rostam.'
segments.s437-g55: چنان بد که is a conventional poetic sequence meaning 'so (it was) that...' (چنان = 'such/so', بد here functions idiomatically in place of بود; که introduces the result clause). هر سال یک چرم گاو is a temporal and quantity phrase: 'each year one cow-hide' (هر سال = every year; یک = one; چرم گاو = cowhide).
segments.s437-g56: ز کابل introduces the source/location 'from Kabul.' همی again gives a continued/inchoative flavor. خواستی is the past/pluperfect/direct past sense of خواستن here meaning 'demanded/required' (he exacted). باژ و ساو are place names in the list of places furnishing the tribute; ساو is the place Saveh, while باژ is dialectal/poetic and treated here as a toponym.
segments.s437-g1: چنین گوید is an inverted, literary clause: چنین ('thus') precedes the verb گوید ('says') — a present/frequentative poetic form meaning 'says' or 'speaks'. آن پیر is a definite noun phrase ('that/ the old man'), with آن as a demonstrative/article and پیر the noun 'old man/elder'. دانش‌پژوه is in apposition to پیر ('scholar'), so the whole segment means 'thus says that old scholar.'
segments.s437-g2: This is a series of descriptive nouns/epithets applied to the same person: هنرمند ('skilful, artful') then و (conjunction) گوینده ('speaker/orator'), then و با شکوه ('and with splendor' = 'majestic, splendid'). با + noun (شکوه) functions adjectivally here ('with splendor' = 'magnificent'). The coordination links the attributes to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s437-g3: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') modifying the previous person. در پرده بد is a prepositional phrase 'in/behind a bad/ill-omened curtain' (noun + adjective order). زال را uses the object/definite marker را after the name 'Zāl' to indicate the beneficiary/possessor in this construction, and بنده‌ای ('a servant') is indefinite because of the suffix -ای; the segment describes that 'for Zāl there was a servant' (classical word order places such phrases before the verbs that enumerate the roles).
segments.s437-g4: نوازنده رود is a nominal phrase 'player of the rud' (نوازنده 'player/musician' + رود 'rud, a string instrument'); classical Persian often omits an explicit genitive connector here. و گوینده‌ای adds 'and an orator/speaker' with the indefinite -ای. The sequence lists three roles possessed/associated with Zāl in the preceding clause.
segments.s437-g5: This is a compact past narrative: کنیزک ('the little maidservant') is the subject. پسر زاد is a verbal phrase with زاد (root زاییدن) meaning 'gave birth' or 'bore a son' (پسر as the object 'a son'). روزی یکی is the temporal/indefinite adverbial 'one day / a certain day' (poetic placement after the verb is normal).
segments.s437-g6: که introduces a relative clause modifying the newborn ('who'). ازماه is a comparative/simile 'with respect to the moon' (moon = a poetic image of beauty/brightness), پیدا نبود is past/negative 'was not visible/appeared not to be', and اندکی ('a little') is a limiting adverb placed after the verb for emphasis: 'did not show even a little of the moon.'
segments.s437-g7: به بالا is a directional/adverbial phrase 'upwards / looked up' (literary use often omits an explicit verb and lets the next clause complete the sense). و links to the following nominal idea; دیدار here means 'sight' or 'the seeing/meeting' and سام سوار is the name plus epithet 'Sam the rider'. Together the segment sets up 'upon looking up and seeing Sam the rider...'.
segments.s437-g8: ازو is 'because of him / at his sight' (از + او fused), شاد شد is the past tense 'became glad' (شد as past auxiliary/verb), and دوده نامدار is the subject in inverted poetic order: 'Doudé (دوده) the renowned' — the name follows the verb, which is common in Persian poetry. Thus: 'At his sight, the famed Doudé became glad.'
segments.s437-g25: همه سیستان is the subject phrase 'all of Sistan' (whole-region + noun). زو here is a contracted, poetic form of از او ('from/of him' or 'on his account') giving the cause; شود is the present/future‑style verb 'becomes' (third singular), and پرخروش is a predicate adjective 'turbulent/raging' describing the subject. Word order: subject → (causal phrase) → verb → predicative adjective.
segments.s437-g26: همه شهر ایران is another subject phrase 'every/all the cities of Iran' (همه modifies شهر). برآید is a simple present/archaic present 'arise/appear' (third singular); به here marks direction/entering into a state ('into' / 'towards'), and جوش is a noun 'boiling/surge' used figuratively—'to rise into a boil/ferment.' The clause describes a collective upheaval.
segments.s437-g27: شود تلخ is 'becomes bitter' (شود = 'become', تلخ = 'bitter' used figuratively). ازو (از + او) gives the causal/referential link 'because of him/it' or 'from that', and روز is 'day/time' as the thing that becomes bitter. بر هر کسی is a prepositional phrase 'upon every person' (بر = upon, هر کسی = every one) marking the experiencer of that bitterness.
segments.s437-g28: ازان پس is a temporal sequence 'after that/thereupon' (ازان = from that, پس = then/after). به گیتی is a locational phrase 'in/for the world' (به here marking relation to the world), نماند is the negative past/present 'did not remain / is not left', and بسی means 'much/enough'—here 'not much remained.' The clause states the resulting scarcity in the world.
segments.s437-g29: غمی گشت is 'became a grief' (غمی = grief as subject/complement, گشت = became). زان کار 'from/for that deed' gives the cause or source (زان = from that, کار = deed/affair). دستان سام places 'Sam' with دستان (here literal 'his hands' or 'Sam's hands' used idiomatically to indicate Sam personally/ his state); together the phrase says Sam was made sorrowful by that action.
segments.s437-g30: ز دادار گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'from the Creator of the world' (ز = from, دادار = the Creator, گیتی = the world). همی before the verb is an archaic/poetic continuous/affirmative particle (here giving force to the verb), برد is the verb 'took/won/gained', and نام is the object 'name' in the sense 'fame/reputation'. The clause means he obtained renown from the divine source.
segments.s437-g31: به یزدان چنین گفت is 'to the gods he said thus' (به یزدان = to the gods, چنین = thus/so, گفت = said). کای is a vocative 'O' addressing someone, and رهنمای is the noun 'guide' addressed directly. The clause frames a speech to the divine addressee.
segments.s437-g32: تو داری is 'you hold/possess' (تو = you, داری = you have/hold). سپهر روان را is the object phrase 'the moving/flowing heavens' (سپهر = sky/heavens, روان = flowing/moving or 'the sky in motion', را marks it as definite object). به پای is a locative/comparative phrase 'at your foot(s)' or 'at your feet' indicating subjection or control. The clause is a direct address stating the addressee has the heavens underfoot.
segments.s437-g177: که‌گر is an archaic conditional 'if'. The phrase follows Persian word order where the object phrase 'نام تو' ('your name') precedes the verb 'برنویسم' ('I write' / 'let me write'); برنویسم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive form of 'to write' with the prefix بر (poetic). The preposition بر + آب ('on the water') marks the locus of writing.
segments.s437-g178: به کابل is a locative/directional phrase ('to/in Kabul'). نیابد is a negative 3rd-person present/subjunctive/optative form of یافتن ('find/attain') giving the sense 'does not/shall not find'; in Persian a negative verb before a noun often yields the meaning 'no one' (hence کس here functions as 'no one'). آرام و خواب are two coordinated nouns ('peace/quiet and sleep').
segments.s437-g179: The initial که is a complement/relative particle ('that' / 'who') introducing the clause; یارد is the noun 'comrade' or 'companion' and serves as the clause's subject. The second که again introduces the relative clause 'who comes'; پیش تو آید is literally 'comes before you' (پیش = before/in front of, تو = you, آید = comes). به جنگ is a directional/complement phrase 'into battle' placed after the verb.
segments.s437-g180: وگر is a conditional 'if' (or 'but if'). تو بجنبی uses the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form بجنبی ('you move/stir'); Persian often uses the subjunctive to express contingent acts. The following که introduces a result/relative clause: سازد is 3rd-person singular 'he makes/causes' and درنگ is the object 'delay/pause' — together 'he will cause delay.'
segments.s437-g181: برآنم is a fixed spoken/poetic phrase meaning 'I am of that mind / I intend / I am resolved'; that is followed by the complementizer که introducing the subordinate clause. او is the subject 'he'; زین here is used in the subordinate clause (in context literary/archaic, literally 'of the saddle' or 'about this saddle'); پشیمان شده‌ست is a compound present-perfect/passive-like form 'has become regretful' (شده‌ست = 'has become/been').
segments.s437-g182: وزین here is best read as an archaic/conjunctive form (و + زین) linking to the previous clause ('and / and because of this'); رفتنم is the nominalized verb with the 1st-person possessive suffix (-م) meaning 'my going' or 'my departure'; سوی درمان is the directional phrase 'toward cure/remedy'; شده‌ست is the same present-perfect 'has been/done', so syntactically this clause says the speaker's going toward a cure has been effected by/for this reason. (Note: the lemma senses listed do not include the conjunctive/demonstrative reading of وزین, so that element is contextually irregular.)
segments.s437-g183: بیارد here is an archaic jussive/imperative/optative verb form meaning 'bring' / 'let him bring' (verb root related to آوردن); in poetry verbs can appear before their objects for emphasis. کنون is the adverb 'now'; پیش is the preposition 'before / in front of'; خواهشگران is the plural noun 'petitioners / supplicants' and is the group before whom something should be presented.
segments.s437-g184: ز کابل is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from Kabul' (ز = from). گزیده is an adjective 'chosen / selected' modifying the following noun; فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'numerous / abundant' intensifying the selection; سران is the plural noun 'leaders / chiefs' (literally 'heads'), the noun phrase 'گزیده فراوان سران' = 'many chosen chiefs'. Word order here (adjectives before noun) reflects literary style.
segments.s437-g121: Subject 'تو' (you) is fronted; 'از تخمهٔ سام' is a genitive/prepositional phrase 'of the seed/offspring of Sam' introduced by 'از'. 'نیرم' is a predicative adjective 'weak/soft' placed before the copula, and 'نه‌ای' is the archaic/poetic negative copula ('are not'). The overall order is inverted for poetic effect: 'You of Sam's seed weak are not.'
segments.s437-g122: 'برادر نه‌ای' is a short predicate clause: 'you are not a brother' with 'نه‌ای' as the negative copula. 'خوی' means 'kin/kinship' and the enclitic 'ش' (‑ash) marks 'his' (attached to the noun), followed by 'رستم' to specify whose kin: 'not Rostam's kinsman.' The repeated 'نه‌ای' negates each nominal predicate.
segments.s437-g123: The verb 'نکردست' (an older/past perfect negative form) governs the clause; 'یاد از تو' = 'remembrance/memory of you' is the object. 'دستان سام' ('the tales/deeds of Sam') appears after the verb as the topical/possessive phrase. The clause literally reads: 'Sam's tales have made no remembrance of you.'
segments.s437-g124: 'برادر' is a vocative 'O brother.' 'ز تو' = 'from you' (standard of source). 'کی برد' is an interrogative/perfect idiom 'who/when ever carried/gained' with 'کی' functioning as 'who/ever' here; 'نیز نام' = 'even (or likewise) a name' (i.e. any fame). The sentence is rhetorical: 'Who has ever won even a name from you?'
segments.s437-g125: 'تو' is the subject. The comparative construction uses 'از' to introduce the standard: 'از چاکران کمتری' = 'less/fewer than the attendants/servants.' 'بر درش' means 'at his door/gate' where 'بر' = 'on/at' and 'در' = 'door' with the enclitic 'ش' = 'his.' The adjective 'کمتری' functions as the comparative 'less/fewer.'
segments.s437-g126: This clause has an inverted order: object‑phrase + verb + subject. 'برادر' = 'brother' is the predicative noun; 'نخواند' is the past negative of 'خواندن' used here in the sense 'to call/summon' ('did not call'). 'ترا' is the accusative 'you' expressed as the enclitic 'ت' plus object-marking 'را'. 'مادرش' at the end is the subject 'his mother.' Together: 'His mother did not call you brother.'
segments.s437-g127: 'ز گفتار او' = 'from/because of his speech/words' (z = from/because of). 'تنگ‌دل شد' is a compound predicate: 'تنگ‌دل' (narrow‑hearted, sorrowful/resentful) + 'شد' (became) — a past change of state. The personal name 'شغاد' appears in final position (poetic inversion) as the subject who underwent this change.
segments.s437-g128: 'برآشفت' is a past verb 'became angry/got enraged.' The conjunction 'و' links the next clause. 'سر سوی زابل نهاد' is an idiomatic verbl phrase: literally 'put (his) head toward Zabul' where 'سر' = 'head', 'سوی' = 'toward', 'زابل' = place name, and 'نهاد' is past of 'نهادن' 'placed/set' — idiomatically 'he set off for Zabul' or 'turned his face toward Zabul.' The subject (he/Shaghad) is implied from the previous clause.
segments.s437-g9: A coordinated subject phrase: ستاره‌شناسان (plural) + و + کنداوران (plural). ستاره‌شناسان is a compound noun meaning people who study/observe stars (here 'astronomers/astrologers'); کنداوران is the plural of کنداور ('investigator, researcher' or literal 'excavator'). The conjunction و simply links the two groups.
segments.s437-g10: A prepositional source phrase z + place(s) followed by a noun phrase describing those people. ز کشمیر و کابل = 'from Kashmir and Kabul' (z is the poetic preposition 'from'). گزیده is a past‑participle/adjective 'chosen/selected' modifying سران 'leaders, chiefs'. Word order here places the adjective before the noun (گزیده سران = 'chosen chiefs'); the whole phrase qualifies the previously mentioned groups as coming from those regions.
segments.s437-g11: Parallel prepositional phrases introduced by ز (z) before each group for poetic balance: ز آتش‌پرست و ز یزدان‌پرست = 'from the fire‑worshippers and from the God‑worshippers.' Each compound word (آتش‌پرست, یزدان‌پرست) is an agent noun meaning 'one who worships X'.
segments.s437-g12: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they departed/they went (forth)'. The following prepositional phrase با زیج رومی به دست attaches as instrument/possession: با + object (زیج رومی = 'a Roman/Byzantine zij, i.e. astronomical tables') and the idiom به دست = 'in (their) hand, carrying'. Together: 'they went forth carrying a Roman zij in hand.'
segments.s437-g13: گرفتند is a past plural verb 'they took' used here idiomatically as 'they undertook/made'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying the verb. شمار سپهر is a noun phrase 'the count/reckoning of the heavens' (شمار = count; سپهر = sky/heavens). Overall: 'they made a complete count of the heavens.'
segments.s437-g14: Relative clause introduced by که ('which/that') modifying شمار سپهر. دارد is the (present/habitual) verb 'has' or 'shows'. بران is an enclitic/contracted prepositional phrase (بر آن = 'upon that/for that'). کودک خرد = 'the small/young child' (کودک 'child', خرد here 'small' rather than 'wisdom'). مهر here functions as 'love/affection'. So the clause means roughly 'which has for that little child love' = 'which shows/bestows love upon that young child.'
segments.s437-g15: A compact clause: ستاره (here used metonymically for 'stargazers' or 'celestial observers/astrologers') + شمرکان (a plural agent/population name, 'the people/followers of Shamar' or 'the Shamarites') form a possessive/attributive phrase. شگفتی is the object 'a wonder/marvel', and بدید is the past verb 'they saw'. So: 'the stargazers of Shamarkan saw a marvel.'
segments.s437-g16: همی is an adverb 'continually/kept on'. The formulaic phrase این بدان آن بدین is poetic/paratactic: the particles b- + verbs (here written separated as ب + دان and ب + دین in the word list) form the verbs بدان / بدین meaning 'consider this to be that / consider that to be this' — i.e. they repeatedly turned things over in interpretation. بنگرید (present infinitive/imperative form here functioning as a finite verb in narrative) means 'they looked/gazed.' Overall: 'continually they looked, taking this for that and that for this.'
segments.s437-g33: به هر کار is a prepositional phrase 'in/for every affair' (به + هر + کار). پشت و پناهم are two coordinated nouns 'back/support and my refuge' with the possessive -م on پناه; the final توی is the locative 'in' (توی) linking those nouns to an implicit second-person referent ('in you' or 'is in you'). The copula ('is') is omitted, as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s437-g34: نماینده رای و راهم is a coordinated noun phrase: 'representative (نماینده) of counsel/opinion (رای) and my way/path (راهم)'. The final توی again functions as the locative 'in' applying to the whole coordinate phrase ('in you'). The verb 'to be' is omitted; the phrase thus reads idiomatically as 'in counsel you are my representative and in my path you are my guide.'
segments.s437-g35: سپهر آفریدی is a simple past second-person clause 'you created the sky' (آفریدی = you created). The conjunction و links the following phrase اختر همان 'that very/that same star', where همان postmodifies اختر for emphasis ('that very star').
segments.s437-g36: همه نیکویی باد is an optative construction: باد is the optative particle 'may it be', so 'همه نیکویی باد' = 'may all goodness be'. ما را گمان follows as the experiencer/possessive phrase 'our belief/hope', with ما را marking 'to/for us' or topicalizing 'us' (classical word order places the possessor/recipient before the head noun گمان). Overall: 'May all goodness be our belief.'
segments.s437-g37: بجز introduces an exception list 'except/other than', followed by nouns کام و آرام و خوبی. The clause closes with مباد, an optative/negative particle ('may it not be') that here functions idiomatically with بجز to express 'let there be nothing except ...' — a standard classical formulaic phrasing.
segments.s437-g38: ورا نام کرد is an older-order phrase where ورا (= او را) is the object pronoun 'him/it' preceding the noun نام and the verb کرد ('he made/did'); together 'he gave him a name' or 'he named him.' آن precedes the title سپهبد ('that spahbed') and شغاد is the proper name; کرد is past-tense 3rd singular 'he called/named.'
segments.s437-g39: همی داشت is a past-continuous idiom 'was keeping/continued to have' (همی + داشت used as progressive). مادر is the subject 'the mother'. چو شد سیر شیر is a temporal subordinate clause 'when/until he became sated with milk' (چو = when; شد = became; سیر = satiety; شیر = milk). The explicit object (the child) is omitted as typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s437-g40: دلارام و گوینده و یادگیر is a coordinate series of nouns/epithets: the proper name دلارام followed by two descriptive nouns 'speaker' (گوینده) and 'learner' (یادگیر), joined by و. They function appositively or as titles; no copula is required in Persian.
segments.s437-g153: ازین پس is a fixed adverbial phrase (از + این + پس) meaning “from now on/after this.” نگوییم is first-person-plural negative/subjunctive of گفتن (“we do not say / let us not say”); it precedes the clause introduced by کو, which here functions as the interrogative/exclamative introducing quoted content (“Where is...?”). رستمست is an archaic poetic contraction of رستم است (Rostam + copula 'is'); the clause کو رستمست is the quoted question/phrase that نگوییم negates.
segments.s437-g154: نه introduces a negation (here continuing the prohibition from the previous clause). زو is the poetic contraction of از او (“from him”); مردی and گوهر ما are two coordinated nouns (‘manliness’ / ‘manhood’ and ‘our jewel/essence’), linked by و. کمست is an archaic fused form of کم است (“is little/ is lacking”). Together the phrase says that we should not say that, on his account, our manhood and jewel are diminished.
segments.s437-g155: نه is a negative particle here (continuing the negation). فرزند زالی is a genitive phrase ‘the son of Zāl’ (زالی = Zāl’s epithet); مرا را (here shown as م + را) functions as the indirect object / topicalizer ‘to me’ or ‘upon me’ and گفت is the past verb ‘said’. نیز adds ‘also/likewise’ — so the whole segment reports that he likewise said (to me) ‘not the son of Zāl’.
segments.s437-g156: وگر is an archaic conditional ‘if / but if’. هستی is the noun ‘existence/being’ used with او to mean ‘if he exists’. او خود combines the pronoun and the emphatic self (‘he himself’). نیرزد is the negative present/subjunctive of ارزیدن (‘is worth’) meaning ‘is not worth / does not deserve’; به چیز = ‘to/for a thing’ = ‘anything’. The clause therefore means ‘and if he exists, he himself is not worth a thing.’
segments.s437-g157: ازان is the archaic form of از آن (‘from that/for that reason’). مهتران is the plural noun ‘chiefs/leaders’ and functions with ازان to mark cause (‘because of those chiefs’). شد is the past tense auxiliary ‘became’; دلم is ‘my heart’ (دل + enclitic م); پر ز درد is a predicate phrase ‘full of pain/sorrow’ (پر = full, ز = of, درد = pain). Word order places the causative prepositional phrase before the verb and predicate, typical in epic diction.
segments.s437-g158: ز کابل is the prepositional phrase ‘from Kabul’ (ز = from). براندم is first-person past ‘I drove away / I sent off / I removed’ (برانداختن/برانیدن in poetic usage). دو رخساره is ‘two faces/pale-faced ones’ (دو + noun); رخساره is ‘face/complexion’ and the adjective زرد follows and describes them (‘pale/wan’). The whole clause says that from Kabul the speaker drove off two pale-faced persons.
segments.s437-g159: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بشنید is the simple past ‘he heard’ (third-person). The subject name رستم follows and then the result verb برآشفت (‘grew angry/was enraged’) — the prefix بر- adds an intensive/at-once sense to آشفتن. و گفت links the reaction to the reported speech (‘and said’). The clause order (temporal clause → subject → result) is typical in epic prose/poetry.
segments.s437-g160: که introduces reported content (‘that’). هرگز is a strong negator ‘never’. نماند is the present/future-subjunctive sense of ماندن used here with هرگز to mean ‘will not remain / never remains’. سخن is ‘word/speech’. در نهفت (در + نهفت) = ‘hidden/in concealment’ (نهفت is past participle/adjective ‘hidden’). Together: ‘that no word will ever remain hidden.’
segments.s437-g65: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' and introduces reported speech; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. با + noun marks the addressee ('to/with the king of Kabul'), and نهان ('secretly/in private') qualifies the manner of the speaking. Word order is typical Persian: adverb + verb + prepositional phrase; نهان can modify the verb phrase as 'in private'.
segments.s437-g66: که introduces the content clause (that / 'that I...'). من is the pronoun 'I'. سیر is a predicate adjective/noun meaning 'sated/tired' and گشتم is the past form of گشتن meaning 'I became / I grew'; together سیر گشتم = 'I have become sated/weary'. ز (poetic form of از) marks source/reason ('of/from'), and کار جهان = 'the affairs of the world'.
segments.s437-g67: برادر here is a direct address or reference 'brother'. که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. او را combines the pronoun with the definite object/topic marker را (here marking the person being spoken about). ز من = 'with/from me' (i.e. 'of me' / 'before me'), شرم نیست = 'there is no shame' (literally 'shame does not exist'). The clause means 'a brother who has no shame before me.'
segments.s437-g68: مرا (here shown as م + را in the tokenization) functions as the experiencer/topic 'for me' with an existential/negated predicate: 'for me there is no ...'. سوی او = 'toward him' (direction/relationship). راه و آزرم نیست = 'there is no way/path and no restraint/modesty'; آزرم is an archaic/poetic word meaning modesty/shame. Overall: 'I have neither a way to approach him nor any modesty/respect toward him.'
segments.s437-g69: The repeated چه is exclamatory ('what/how ...!'). مهتر is an honorific/adjective meaning 'elder/greater/distinguished' modifying برادر ('brother'). The second چه + بیگانه‌ای contrasts with the first: 'what a stranger!' The construction is rhetorical, listing contrasts with exclamatory چه.
segments.s437-g70: Again, چه is exclamatory. فرزانه 'wise/sage-like' modifies مردی ('man'); the phrase 'فرزانه مردی' = 'a wise man'. The final 'چه دیوانه‌ای' is another exclamation 'what a madman you are!' with the vocative/exclamatory ای at the end. The pattern contrasts praise and reproach in short clauses.
segments.s437-g71: بسازیم is first-person plural optative/subjunctive 'let us make/let us contrive' (a hortative proposal). و is 'and'. او را is the definite object marker with 'him'. به دام آوریم: به دام = 'into a trap/snare' and آوریم (subjunctive) 'let us bring/cause to come' — together 'let us bring him into a trap'. The verbs are hortative/suggestive (we will/let us...).
segments.s437-g72: به گیتی = 'in/to the world' (locative/goal). بدین کار is instrumental 'by this deed' (بدین = 'by/with this', کار = 'act/deed'). نام آوریم is first-person plural causative/subjunctive 'let us bring/name' meaning 'we will make (someone) a name' i.e. 'make him famous'. The line says they will catch him and thereby make him (in)famous in the world.
segments.s437-g97: براندازه is an adverbial phrase meaning “proportionate to / the size of” placed before the nouns it qualifies. رستم و رخش are two coordinated proper nouns (Rostam and his horse Rakhsh). ساز here is poetic/archaic in force: it functions as an imperative/command-like verb “make/prepare” (ساز کن/ساختن) — a terse poetic form meaning “make it so” that attaches to the preceding phrase.
segments.s437-g98: به introduces a locative/directional phrase (“to/at”). بن در is a compound meaning ‘the base/bottom of the door/gate’ (بن = base; در = door/gate). نشان is a noun ‘mark/token’ that takes تیغهای دراز (‘long blades/swords’) as its descriptive genitive phrase (تیغهای = blades/swords, دراز = long). Word order places the locative (به بن در) before the object (نشان تیغهای دراز).
segments.s437-g99: همان is a demonstrative ‘the same / that’ placed before the nouns it points to. نیزه و حربه are two coordinated nouns ‘spear and weapon’. آبگون is a postpositive adjective (poetic order) meaning ‘bluish / water-colored’, modifying the preceding nouns.
segments.s437-g100: سنان here refers to spear-points/spears and is followed by the directional phrase از بر, literally ‘from/on the outside’ (i.e., pointing outward). و links the second specification: نیزه زیر اندرون = ‘spears beneath/inside’ (زیر = under/beneath; اندرون = inside, an archaic poetic term). The phrase describes orientation: points toward the outside, shafts toward the interior/below.
segments.s437-g101: اگر begins a conditional clause ‘if’. صد precedes the verb in classical/poetic word order and specifies quantity (‘a hundred’). کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form used in conditions (‘if you do / if you make/dig’). چاه is the object ‘well/pit’; بهتر ز is a comparative phrase ‘better than’, with ز used poetically as ‘than’. The overall structure is conditional and comparative.
segments.s437-g102: چو is an archaic conjunction here meaning ‘if/when’. خواهی is the 2nd-person present of خواستن ‘you wish/want’; که is the complementizer introducing the subordinate clause. آسوده گردی = ‘you become/are at ease’ where گردی is a verb form meaning ‘become’ (subjunctive/optative flavor in classical Persian). ز رنج = ‘from suffering/hardship’ gives the source from which one is relieved.
segments.s437-g103: بجای is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning ‘instead of / in place (of)’. آر is an archaic/poetic verbal form here with imperative force (‘bring’ or ‘set in place’) rather than the listed senses — it’s an old usage; صد مرد places the numeral before the noun (‘a hundred men’). نیرنگ ساز is a compound: نیرنگ (stratagem) + ساز (maker/one who fashions) yields ‘makers of stratagems’ = ‘tricksters/men of craft’ (agentive compound).
segments.s437-g104: بکن is an imperative ‘do/make’ (here: dig). چاه is the direct object ‘pit/well’. و links the following clause. بر باد مگشای is a conventional optative/negative formula: باد is used optatively (‘may [it]…’), مگشای is a negative imperative ‘do not open/reveal’ (مـ + گشای), so together ‘may the wind not open/reveal’. راز is the object ‘secret’. The sequence is a command combined with a wish that the secret remain unrevealed.
segments.s437-g145: ازو is a prepositional pronoun meaning “from him / of him.” نیکویی بد is a paired nominal phrase meaning “goodness and badness” (i.e. both kindness and harm); مرا is split orthographically into م + را here (object pronoun + Persian definite/object marker ra) and marks the patient “me.” پیش ازین is a temporal adverbial “before this / previously.” The whole segment states that he (from him) had done both good and ill to the speaker earlier.
segments.s437-g146: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” دیدی is 2nd-person singular past “you saw.” Again the object appears split as م + را (m + ra) marking “me.” خواندی is past tense ‘you called/uttered’ (here ‘you cried out / praised’), and آفرین is the interjection “bravo / well done.” Together: “When you saw me you cried ‘Bravo!’ ”
segments.s437-g147: کنون is the adverb “now.” می‌خورد is the present/habitual form of خوردن “he eats / he consumes” (here idiomatically ‘feasts or takes his ease / plunders’). جنگ سازد is a present-tense third-person clause “he makes/causes war” (سازد = ‘makes’), and همی is the old/poetic continuous/adverbial particle meaning an ongoing or habitual action (“keeps on / still”).
segments.s437-g148: سر is the noun “head” used metaphorically; از here means “from / over” in the comparative sense, and هر کسی = “everyone / each person.” برفرازد is a present/past-form verb (3rd person) meaning “he lifts up / exalts,” and the trailing همی again gives an ongoing or habitual sense. The clause literally says he lifts his head above every person (i.e. exalts himself over everyone).
segments.s437-g149: مرابر is a fused/poetic form corresponding to مرا بر (me + the preposition بر), so the segment reads ‘(he) made me… before/at (the assembly).’ سر انجمن is the locative phrase ‘before / at the assembly’ (سر can indicate the position or presence before the assembly), خوار کرد is past-tense ‘made (me) despised / humiliated.’ Overall: ‘He humiliated me in front of the assembly.’
segments.s437-g150: همان = “that same”; گوهر here means “essence / character” (not only a jewel); بد = “bad / evil.” پدیدار کرد is a two-word verbal phrase ‘made appear / revealed’ in the past. So the clause says he brought out or exposed that same evil nature.
segments.s437-g151: همی گفت is an iterative/habitual past expression meaning “he kept saying / he would say.” تا کی is the question phrase “how long?” ازین = از + ین (‘of/from this’ ; ین is the orthographic variant of این meaning “this”). باژ و ساو appear as two items joined by و; the text can be read as nouns or names (the manuscript’s lexical options allow reading باژ as “cry/noise” or as a name, and ساو as a proper name/place). The segment reports his repeated complaint ‘How long (will) these Baž and Sāv…?’
segments.s437-g152: که is the complementizer/relative ‘that / for’; با = “with.” سیستان = the region Sīstān. ما = “we/us.” نداریم is present-tense ‘we do not have / we lack.’ تاو in the manuscript appears as a short word whose intended meaning here is likely a pronominal ‘him / such a one’ (i.e. ‘we have no one like him’), but the provided lexical senses do not include that reading, so its precise lexical identity is uncertain. Taken together the clause says essentially: ‘for we in Sīstān have no such (man).’

sentences.s437-s37: They spoke, and the two stood matched; in thought they surpassed the moon.

sentences.s437-s39: One night, when the sun rose from the mountain, sleep did not come upon the two.
sentences.s437-s40: that we should lessen his name from the world, and fill Zāl's heart and eyes with dew (tears).
sentences.s437-s69: The hero became glad at seeing him; when he looked upon him he (found him) wise and bright‑souled.
sentences.s437-s70: He said thus: 'From the seed/line of Sam no lion is born except one mighty and brave.'
sentences.s437-s71: How is your affair with a Kabulite? What do they say about Rostam of Zabul?
sentences.s437-s72: Thus Shaghād answered Rostam: 'Do not even mention the king of Kabul.'
sentences.s437-s41: Thus Shaghad said to the king of Kabul: “If by this speech we are to render justice, we will indeed render it.”

sentences.s437-s93: Rostam said thus: "This is the road — I myself do not need an army to go to Kabul."
sentences.s437-s94: From Zowāreh that will do: a hundred famed horsemen, and likewise a hundred famed foot soldiers.
sentences.s437-s65: He was going with a few men from Kabul — a man whose heart was full of hatred and whose lips were full of the cold wind.
sentences.s437-s66: He came into the presence of the fortunate father, his heart full of expedients and his head full of hatred.

sentences.s437-s68: He questioned him much and struck him, and immediately then he attacked Piltan.
sentences.s437-s45: Whether before brother or before father, I will call you names and a vile wretch.
sentences.s437-s46: May his head be thrown into turmoil on my account, and may he come to this famous city of mine.
sentences.s437-s47: If such a deed falls to our hands, the wheel of heaven will be under our thumb.
sentences.s437-s48: You keep watch at the hunting‑ground on the road; dig a pit for a while at the hunting‑ground.
sentences.s437-s57: When his head was filled with kingly wine, Shaghād inside was thrown into agitation by ill temper.
sentences.s437-s58: Thus he said to the king of Kabul: 'I am always proud in every assembly.'
sentences.s437-s59: A brother — like Rostam, like Dastan's father — more famed than the man who owns a jewel.
sentences.s437-s60: Thereupon the king of Kabul grew angry and said, 'How many things do you have hidden in your speech?'
sentences.s437-s9: They said to Zāl, Sam the rider: “O you who are a remnant of the lofty stars.”
sentences.s437-s10: We seized and examined the secret of the heavens; the heavens have no love for this child's wisdom.
sentences.s437-s11: When this fair‑faced one reaches manhood, the hour of valor and of tumult arrives.
sentences.s437-s12: He will make Sam's seed weak and ruined; by this scheme he will bring about defeat.
sentences.s437-s21: That year the child raised a mane; Zal sent him to the king of Kabul.
sentences.s437-s22: He became a young man, rising to the height of a tall cypress; a brave horseman, with mace and lasso.
sentences.s437-s23: The commander of Kabul looked at him; indeed the crown and throne of the Kayanids suited him.
sentences.s437-s24: The world rejoiced at his sight; (to) him he gave a daughter for the sake of lineage.
sentences.s437-s85: He chose from the army a worthy man, someone renowned in battle.
